title: Character Frequency Analyzer (letters, all chars, or words)
shortDescription: Count how often each character or word appears in your text. Sortable, with percentages, and optional case sensitivity.
description: Paste any text to get a sorted frequency table. Three modes: all characters, letters/digits only, or whole words. Useful for cryptanalysis, writing analysis, and dataset cleanup.
metaDescription: Free character frequency analyzer. Count letters, all characters, or words. Sortable table with percentages. UTF-8 aware including CJK and emoji.

sections.heading: What's it for
paragraphs: Cryptanalysis: classical ciphers (Caesar, substitution) preserve letter frequencies. English text always has E as the most common letter, then T, A, O, I, N. If you see roughly that distribution in cipher text, you have a substitution. CJK languages have very different distributions but still recognizable.
Writing analysis: spotting overused words is one of the fastest ways to improve drafts. If 'just' or 'really' appears 50 times in a 1000-word essay, you've found a tic to fix.
Dataset cleanup: scanning a CSV column with this tool reveals stray characters, encoding errors, and unexpected casing. Useful before importing data into a stricter system.

sections.heading: Three modes
paragraphs: All characters: includes spaces, punctuation, line breaks, emoji. Best for raw text analysis. Useful when you suspect hidden characters (zero-width space, BOM) corrupting a file.
Letters and digits: filters to only Unicode letters and numbers. Best for traditional letter-frequency analysis (cryptanalysis, language identification).
Words: splits on whitespace and counts whole words. Best for writing analysis and stylistic checking.

sections.heading: What 'case sensitive' does
paragraphs: Off (default): 'A' and 'a' count together. Best for letter-frequency on natural text where case is incidental.

On: 'A' and 'a' count separately. Useful when case is meaningful — programming identifiers, branded terms, or analyzing capitalization patterns. Note: case-insensitive folding uses the locale's lowercase rules; for most languages this is the conventional Unicode case folding.

howTo.name: Paste text
howTo.text: Anything from a sentence to a multi-megabyte file.

howTo.name: Toggle case sensitivity
howTo.text: Off for natural text; on when case is meaningful.

howTo.name: Read the table
howTo.text: Sorted by frequency. Top 50 shown; remaining tail summarized below.

faq.q: Does it work for Japanese, Chinese, Korean text?
faq.a: Yes. Letter mode treats each ideograph as one 'letter', so you get hanzi/kanji frequency. Word mode splits on whitespace, which means CJK text without spaces shows as one giant word — use letter mode for those.

faq.q: What's the most common English letter?
faq.a: 'E' (about 12.7%), then T (9.1%), A (8.2%), O (7.5%), I (7.0%), N (6.7%). Knowing this is the foundation of breaking simple substitution ciphers.
Yes in 'all characters' mode. Letter mode filters them out (they're not letters per Unicode classification).

faq.q: Why are emoji sometimes split into multiple characters?
faq.a: Some emoji are multiple Unicode code points (e.g., flags = two regional indicator letters). The counter follows JavaScript string iteration which respects code points but not all grapheme clusters. For most analysis this is fine.
Not yet — copy-paste the rendered table for now. CSV export is on the roadmap.

faq.q: How many entries does it show?
faq.a: Top 50 in the table. The tail count is summarized at the bottom.

faq.q: Why don't case-insensitive Greek/Turkish results match my expectation?
faq.a: Some languages have unusual case rules (Turkish dotted/dotless I; German ß ↔ SS). We use JavaScript's toLowerCase() which follows the default Unicode case folding — usually fine but can surprise in edge cases.

faq.q: Does the data leave my browser?
